title: Jarvis For Superintendents
content:
# Jarvis for Superintendents — The Value Proposition

## The Concept

You don't build Roche a tool. You give him an AI operating system — a Jarvis — that grows with him. You teach him how to use it. And then you stay in his corner as the person who makes it better over time.

This is "teach a man to fish" meets "keep upgrading his fishing rod."

---

## Why This Is Better Than a One-Time Tool

**Option A (what most consultants do):** Build an automation. Hand it over. Walk away. Client uses it until it breaks or they outgrow it. You get paid once.

**Option B (what you're proposing):** Set up a Jarvis on his machine. Teach him how to think with it. He starts using it daily — meeting notes, email drafts, agendas, decision-making. As he uses it, he discovers new needs. You build tools for those needs. Some of those tools are things you already built for yourself. You roll them out. Every tool you build for ANY client can be deployed to ALL clients.

Option B creates:
- A relationship, not a transaction
- Recurring value (new tools, ongoing support)
- A network effect (10 superintendents all running Jarvis = shared tool library)
- A case study that sells itself (Roche shows his Jarvis to other superintendents)

---

## What Roche's Jarvis Looks Like (Day 1)

A workspace on his laptop (or a private GitHub repo) structured around his actual role:

```
roche-jarvis/
├── CLAUDE.md              # How the AI operates for Roche
├── user/
│   └── USER.md            # Roche's profile: role, priorities, communication style
├── people/
│   ├── board-members/     # School board contacts, preferences, context
│   ├── principals/        # Building principals he manages
│   ├── teachers/          # Key staff he interacts with regularly
│   └── external/          # Other superintendents, state contacts
├── meetings/
│   └── (meeting notes land here, auto-organized by date)
├── artifacts/
│   ├── board-prep/        # Board meeting agendas, presentations, data
│   ├── budgets/           # Budget season documents
│   └── strategic-plans/   # District goals, improvement plans
├── skills/
│   ├── meeting-minutes.md # SOP: how to process meeting notes → minutes + action items
│   ├── agenda-builder.md  # SOP: how to build next week's agenda from open items
│   ├── email-drafter.md   # SOP: how to draft email responses in Roche's voice
│   └── board-prep.md      # SOP: how to prepare for board meetings
└── tools/                 # Web tools you build and deploy
    └── (calendar app, time tracker, etc.)
```

**What he can do on day 1 after setup:**
- Brain dump meeting notes → AI generates minutes with action items
- Say "draft my agenda for next week" → AI pulls from open items across all recent meetings
- Paste an email → AI drafts a response in his voice and tone
- Say "prep me for the board meeting" → AI pulls relevant artifacts and summarizes key talking points

**What he can do after week 2:**
- Add people to his system and the AI knows context for every interaction
- AI reminds him of follow-ups he hasn't done
- AI connects dots across meetings ("this came up in your October board meeting too")

---

## How Remote Collaboration Works

### The Simple Way (V1 — Start Here)
Roche's Jarvis lives in a **private GitHub repo**. Brayan has collaborator access.

- **Roche** uses Claude Code on his laptop to interact with his Jarvis daily
- **Brayan** clones the repo, builds new tools or skills, pushes updates
- Roche pulls the updates (or they auto-sync via git)
- Brayan can see what's in the workspace (meeting notes, people files) only if Roche commits them — Roche controls what's shared

This means:
- Brayan can build a new skill (e.g., "board-prep.md") on his own laptop, test it, and push it to Roche's repo
- Roche gets the update next time he pulls
- No SSH, no remote access to his machine, no security concerns
- Just a shared repo with clear boundaries

### Rolling Out Tools (How One Build Serves Many)
When Brayan builds a web tool (like the calendar/time tracker on Forge):
1. Build it once in his own repo
2. Deploy it to Vercel (or similar) — it's just a URL
3. Share the URL with Roche (and any other superintendent)
4. OR: fork the repo into Roche's GitHub, he deploys his own instance

Example: The Forge calendar app. Brayan already built it. Roche gets his own instance at `roche.forgelegatus.org` or similar. Same codebase, separate data.

### Future State (Multiple Jarvises)
When you have 5 superintendents each running their own Jarvis:
- Shared `skills/` library — one repo of SOPs that all Jarvises reference
- Shared `tools/` — web apps deployed once, used by all
- Individual `people/`, `meetings/`, `artifacts/` — private to each superintendent
- Brayan as the "Jarvis maintainer" — pushes improvements to all clients at once

This is a SaaS model without the SaaS. Each client has their own instance. You maintain the shared layer.

---

## The Teaching Component

### Session 1: Setup + Orientation (2 hours, in person)
- Install Claude Code on Roche's laptop
- Set up the repo structure
- Build USER.md together (interview Roche about his role, priorities, style)
- Show him the basics: how to brain dump, how to ask for drafts, how to use skills
- Run through one real meeting note → minutes → agenda workflow

### Session 2: First Week Check-In (1 hour, Zoom)
- Review what he's used, what confused him
- Add any new skills based on what he actually needed
- Teach him one new capability (e.g., people files, board prep)

### Ongoing: As-Needed Support
- Brayan pushes new tools and skills via GitHub
- Roche texts/emails when he needs something custom
- Brayan builds it and pushes it — sometimes in 20 minutes, sometimes in a day

---

## The Business Model

### What Roche Pays For

**Setup + Training:** One-time fee for:
- Installing and configuring his Jarvis
- Building his USER.md and initial skills
- 2 in-person training sessions
- Getting him self-sufficient

**Ongoing (Optional):** Monthly retainer for:
- New tools and skills pushed to his system
- Priority support when he needs something custom built
- Updates as AI capabilities improve

### How This Scales

| Clients | What Happens |
|---------|-------------|
| 1 (Roche) | Build the system. Learn what a superintendent actually needs. |
| 2-3 | Roche introduces you to his superintendent network. Same system, customized per person. Each new client takes less time because the skills library already exists. |
| 5-10 | Shared tool library is robust. New clients get 80% of the value from existing tools. You're spending most time on custom 20%. |
| 10+ | You have a productized Jarvis setup process. Could train someone else to do the installation. You focus on building new tools. |

### Pricing (Don't Discuss Tomorrow — For Your Reference)

Don't quote any of this tomorrow. This is for when the conversation naturally gets there:

- **Setup + Training:** Position as investment, not expense. When the time comes, you'll know what it's worth based on what Roche tells you about his pain.
- **Monthly:** Same. Let the value become obvious before you name a number.
- **The real leverage:** Every tool you build for Roche, you can deploy to his superintendent friends. Build once, deploy many.

---

## What to Say Tomorrow (and Only This)

After the PD event, privately to Roche:

> "Mr. Roche, you asked me back in March about AI tools for your meetings and emails. I've been thinking about it a lot. I didn't want to just recommend another app. What I've been building is something different — it's a personal AI system that learns how you work and helps you think, not just automate. I built one for myself and it's changed how I operate. I'd love to show you what yours could look like. Can I come back and walk you through it?"

That's it. No price. No proposal. Just: "Can I show you?"

He'll say yes. He always says yes to you.

title: Jeanette Call Plan
content:
# Call Plan: Jeanette Debek (Director of Marketing, Imagi)

## Who Is Imagi?

Imagi (imagilabs.com) is a Swedish-founded ed-tech company that builds AI-powered tools for teaching Python coding and AI literacy in K-12. Their product **imagi Edu** gives teachers lesson plans, worksheets, and a platform where students code pixel art — no CS experience required for teachers. Featured multiple times as App of the Day on Apple App Store. CEO Dora Palfi is actively presenting at ASU+GSV Summit and FETC.

**Their core problem:** 30+ U.S. states now mandate CS in K-12, but schools don't have enough trained teachers. Imagi removes "CS anxiety" for educators.

**Why this matters for Brayan:** Imagi and NGL are solving the same problem from different angles. Imagi provides the tool. NGL provides the human element — the pedagogy, the student engagement, the teacher training. You are natural partners, not competitors.

---

## What Jeanette Cares About (As Director of Marketing)

Jeanette's job is to grow Imagi's user base and prove market demand. She needs:

1. **Real educator feedback** — What do teachers actually say about AI in the classroom? What are their fears? What would make them adopt a tool?
2. **Case studies and proof points** — Stories she can use in marketing. "50 teachers at a PD day went from scared to excited" is gold.
3. **Distribution channels** — How does Imagi get in front of more teachers and districts? Brayan is literally standing in front of 50 teachers tomorrow.
4. **Partnership validation** — Is NGL a credible partner that makes Imagi look good to schools?

---

## Call Structure (15-20 min)

### 1. Open With Value (2 min)

"Jeanette, thank you for making time. I wanted to talk before tomorrow because I think there's a real opportunity for both of us at this event, and I want to make sure I'm gathering the right information for you and Dora."

### 2. Share the Plan (5 min)

Walk her through what's happening tomorrow:

- **The event:** 50 teachers and admins at Woodstock Middle School. Keynote by you, then breakout sessions led by Kristy Zaleta (Danbury Chief of Schools) and Gina Williams (Danbury Director of Technology).

- **Your keynote:** 30 min. You're doing 4 live AI demos showing teachers how AI can be their partner — lesson planning, differentiation, student feedback, explanation testing. You're reframing AI from fear to practical tool.

- **The toolkit:** You built a free resource at forgelegatus.org/woodstock with 6 copy-paste AI drivers. Teachers scan a QR code and walk away with something they can use Monday.

- **Data collection:** Post-event Google Form survey measuring comfort with AI before/after, biggest concerns, interest in follow-up support.

- **The people in the room:** Superintendent Roche (your champion), Kristy Zaleta (controls curriculum for all of Danbury), Gina Williams (controls tech adoption for Danbury). These are decision-makers, not just attendees.

### 3. Ask What She Needs (5 min)

"I want to make sure I'm collecting data that's useful for Imagi too. What would be most valuable for you?"

Possible things she might want:
- Teacher quotes about AI readiness (for marketing copy)
- Data on teacher comfort levels before/after a PD session (for case studies)
- Feedback on what tools teachers wish existed (product validation for Imagi Edu)
- Connections to district decision-makers (Zaleta, Williams, Roche)

**Key question to ask her:**

> "If I could get you one thing from this room of 50 educators, what would be most useful for Imagi's marketing right now?"

Whatever she says, add it to your survey or your listening framework for tomorrow.

### 4. Explore the Partnership (5 min)

This is where you connect the dots:

"I've been thinking about how NGL and Imagi fit together. You have the platform — imagi Edu teaches coding and AI. We have the pedagogy and the human element — we've run pilots in 3 libraries, we have a curriculum framework, and we're now getting in front of districts. What if we explored a partnership where NGL programs incorporate Imagi's tools, and Imagi gets real classroom data and teacher testimonials from our events?"

**What you're offering Imagi:**
- Access to real educators through PD events and pilot programs
- Testimonials and case studies from teachers who try AI in classrooms
- Ground-level feedback on what teachers need (product insights)
- A distribution partner who is already in rooms with district decision-makers

**What you want from Imagi:**
- Validation and credibility (partnership with a funded ed-tech company)
- Their platform as a tool in your curriculum (makes your programs stronger)
- Introduction to their network (conferences, ed-tech ecosystem)
- Potential paid partnership or co-selling arrangement down the line

Don't negotiate anything on this call. Just plant the idea and say: "I'd love to explore this more after the event once I have real data to share."

### 5. Close (2 min)

"I'll send you the survey results and my notes from the event by Monday. If there's anything specific you want me to ask or listen for tomorrow, text me tonight. I want this to be valuable for both of us."

---

## What to Add to Your Survey for Jeanette

Add one question to the Google Form that helps Imagi:

> "What tools or platforms do you currently use (or wish existed) for teaching computer science or AI literacy?" (open text)

This gives Jeanette direct product-market feedback from 50 educators.

---

## What This Call Sets Up Long-Term

Right now, Jeanette and Dora know you as "the kid doing AI education at libraries." After this call and tomorrow's event, they should see you as:

- A partner who delivers real educator data they can't easily get
- Someone who is already in rooms with superintendents and chiefs of schools
- A credible operator who runs events, collects data, and follows through

If Imagi is presenting at ASU+GSV (Dora is), and you can hand them real PD data from Woodstock with teacher testimonials — that's content they can use on stage. That makes you valuable to them in a way that goes beyond a one-time event.

---

## Notes to Update After Call

After the call, update:
- `people/jeanette-debek.md` — what she said, what she wants
- `people/dora-palfi.md` — anything Jeanette shares about Dora's priorities
- The Google Form survey — add any questions she requests
- Your listening framework for tomorrow — any specific signals she wants you to watch for

title: Value Proposition Education
content:
# The Real Value Proposition for Education Leaders

## Why "Save Time" Isn't Enough

The Applied AI playbooks frame value as: "Charge 10-20% of Year 1 business impact." That works for a plumbing company losing $288K/year in missed leads. It does NOT work for a superintendent.

Roche isn't trying to grow revenue. He's not losing leads. He's a public servant managing a K-8 district on a fixed budget. If you walk in and say "I'll save you 5 hours a week on meeting minutes for $2,500," he hears an expense. Another line item. Another thing to justify to the board.

That's the wrong frame entirely.

---

## What Superintendents Actually Care About

From your emails with Roche, here's what he's said in his own words:

> "I continue to say we are preparing students for a world that is ever evolving and to dismiss AI is such a missed opportunity."

> "I think your phrasing of thinking of AI as a 'business partner rather than an employee' was the perfect way to delineate between how we should and should not approach AI."

> "It is so important that teachers and administrators hear from students about how AI can truly enhance instruction."

He's not asking for automation. He's asking for **leadership**. He wants to be the superintendent who got AI right. He wants his teachers confident. He wants his students prepared. He wants to sit in a room with other superintendents and have something to show.

---

## The Three Layers of Value (Beyond Automation)

### Layer 1: Time (Surface Level — What They Expect)
"AI can help you draft meeting minutes, email responses, and agendas faster."

Every AI consultant says this. Every YouTube video teaches this. This is commoditized. It's necessary to demonstrate, but it's not the value.

### Layer 2: Capability (Deeper — What They Don't Know They Need)
"AI doesn't just save you time. It gives you capabilities you didn't have before."

Examples for Roche:
- He can't personalize communication to 50 families. AI can draft 50 personalized parent emails based on each student's progress.
- He can't differentiate PD for every teacher's skill level. AI can create individualized learning paths for each teacher.
- He can't analyze years of student performance data and find patterns. AI can surface insights he'd never see manually.

This isn't automation. This is giving a superintendent superpowers he didn't know existed. This is where teachers go "holy shit" — not because it's faster, but because it was IMPOSSIBLE before.

### Layer 3: Legacy (Deepest — What They Actually Want)
"Your district becomes the model. You become the leader other superintendents learn from."

Roche already wants to convene a group of superintendents around AI. He already sees himself as forward-thinking. The question is: does he have something to show them?

If you build an AI system for Woodstock and it works — Roche doesn't just have a tool. He has a STORY. He sits in that superintendent meeting and says: "Here's what we did. Here's the data. Here's how teachers are using it. Here's what students are producing." Every other superintendent in the room is now behind.

That's not a $2,500 expense. That's positioning. That's legacy.

---

## What This Means for Tomorrow's Conversations

### Don't pitch automation. Demonstrate possibility.

When you demo the Lesson Plan Partner live, don't say "this saves 45 minutes." Say:

> "Notice what just happened. AI asked me about MY students. It didn't give me a generic plan. It gave me something personalized to my classroom. Now imagine every teacher in your district having this — not one-size-fits-all PD, but AI that adapts to each teacher's actual students."

When you demo the Differentiation Engine, don't say "this is faster." Say:

> "That student who understands math but can't show it because of a language barrier? AI just removed that barrier in 60 seconds. The concept stayed hard. The language got accessible. How many students in your district have more in their head than they can put on paper?"

When you demo the Explanation Pressure-Tester, don't say "this helps you practice." Say:

> "What if every teacher could rehearse their hardest lesson before walking into the room? What if they could find out exactly where students would get confused — before it happens? That's not automation. That's making great teachers even greater."

### The line to use with Roche (after the event, privately):

> "Mr. Roche, what I showed today is just the surface. What I've been building goes deeper than prompts and templates. I'm building systems that give educators capabilities they've never had — personalized at scale, data-driven, and designed around how teachers actually work. I'd love to show you what that could look like for Woodstock specifically."

No price. No proposal. Just: "I'd love to show you." Let him ask for more.

---

## How This Changes the Pipeline

### Old frame (expense):
"I'll build you an AI workflow for meeting minutes. $2,500. 2 weeks."
- He hears: cost, line item, budget approval needed
- Risk: he says "I can probably just figure out Granola myself"

### New frame (investment in leadership):
"I want to help Woodstock become the district that figured out AI. Not just for your meetings — for your teachers, your students, and the story you tell other superintendents. Let me show you what I'm thinking."
- He hears: vision, leadership, competitive advantage
- Risk: none — you're offering to SHOW him, not asking for money yet

### When to talk money:
Not tomorrow. Not this week. After you've shown him what's possible — after he's seen the PD event work, after he's seen teachers respond — THEN you say: "I can build this for Woodstock. Here's what it would look like." And at that point, he's not evaluating an expense. He's investing in something he already believes in.

---

## The Deeper Truth

You said it yourself: you're used to automation tools. Most people aren't. But showing someone Granola or ChatGPT prompts — that's a one-time "wow" that fades. What doesn't fade is:

1. **The teacher who tried the Explanation Pressure-Tester and realized AI found a gap in her lesson she'd been missing for years.** That's a capability shift. She's a different teacher now.

2. **The ELL student who finally demonstrated mastery because AI separated the concept from the language barrier.** That's not automation. That's equity.

3. **The superintendent who sat in a room with 10 peers and was the only one who could show real AI integration data.** That's not a $2,500 project. That's career-defining.

Your value isn't "I'll automate your tasks." Your value is "I'll show you what you didn't know was possible, and then I'll build it for you."

That's what makes people say: "I need to talk to that person about AI."

title: Holy Shit Moments
content:
# The "Holy Shit" Moments — What Teachers Don't Know Yet

## Why Prompts Aren't Enough

Every AI PD session gives teachers prompts. Prompts are commoditized. Teachers can Google "AI prompts for lesson planning" and get 500 results. That's not the value.

The value is what you DISCOVERED by actually teaching with AI in real classrooms for 3 months. You have field data that no prompt template can replace. Here's what you know that they don't:

---

## Insight 1: "The Ego Barrier"
**What you found:** Teachers' biggest blocker isn't technical skill — it's ego. They view their lived experience as threatened by AI. They think: "I've been teaching 20 years. A machine can't replace what I know."

**What they need to hear:** "You're right. It can't. But your 20 years of experience PLUS AI makes you unstoppable. The teacher who uses AI isn't being replaced — they're becoming the most powerful version of themselves. Your lived experience is the thing AI doesn't have. When you combine the two, nobody can compete with you."

**Why this is a holy shit moment:** It reframes AI from a threat to a power-up. No PD session says this. They all say "AI is a tool." You're saying "AI makes YOUR experience more valuable, not less."

---

## Insight 2: "Students Already Know More Than You Think"
**What you found:** 4th and 5th graders arrived at your pilot already using AI — but badly. They were treating it as an answer machine. After just 4 sessions, those same students were:
- Disagreeing with AI outputs
- Revising their own prompts unprompted
- Saying "wait, that's wrong" to ChatGPT
- Explaining WHY they agreed or disagreed with AI

**What teachers need to hear:** "Your students are already using AI. Right now. The question isn't whether they'll use it — that ship has sailed. The question is: are they using it like an employee (do my homework) or like a partner (help me think)? If nobody teaches them the difference, they default to employee mode. That's what cheating looks like. But when someone teaches them the partner mindset, they become BETTER thinkers than they were before AI."

**Why this is a holy shit moment:** Teachers think they're protecting students by keeping AI out. You show them they're actually leaving students unprotected.

---

## Insight 3: "AI Made Thinking VISIBLE for the First Time"
**What you found:** Before-and-after mind maps showed something unexpected. AI didn't replace thinking — it made thinking visible in a way worksheets never could. When students had to explain their reasoning TO AI, and then evaluate AI's response, and then revise their own thinking — every step was captured. You could literally see the growth on paper.

**What teachers need to hear:** "The hardest part of teaching is knowing what's happening inside a student's head. AI makes that visible. When a student writes a prompt, revises it, evaluates the response, and explains what changed — you can see their thinking in real time. This isn't replacing assessment. This is the best assessment tool you've never used."

**The live demo for this:** Show a before/after. Show a student's first prompt (vague, one-line) next to their final prompt (specific, detailed, with context). That growth is VISIBLE. No standardized test shows you that.

---

## Insight 4: "The Question Quality = Life Quality Insight"
**What you found:** Students who asked better questions got dramatically better results from AI. This wasn't just about prompting — it was about thinking. A student who asked "tell me about soccer" got garbage. A student who asked "I want to learn how professional soccer players train their speed, explain it for a 5th grader" got something useful.

**What teachers need to hear:** "We accidentally discovered something. Teaching students to ask better questions of AI taught them to ask better questions of EVERYTHING. The skill transfers. A student who learns to be specific, to state their goal, to give context — that student is better at asking questions in class, in life, in job interviews 10 years from now. AI is the training ground for the most important skill in the world: asking the right question."

**Why this is a holy shit moment:** This reframes AI literacy from "tech skill" to "life skill." Every teacher in that room cares about teaching kids to think. You just showed them AI is the fastest path to that goal.

---

## Insight 5: "The ELL Breakthrough"
**What you found (from Roche conversation):** AI can separate concept mastery from language proficiency. A student who understands fractions but can't express it in English can interact with AI in their comfort zone and demonstrate mastery. AI adapts to THEIR language level while keeping the thinking demands high.

**What teachers need to hear:** "One of our students understood math concepts deeply but couldn't show it on a written test because of language barriers. When she used AI, she could explore the concept at her level, in her way. AI didn't lower the bar — it removed the barrier that had nothing to do with the skill being tested. How many of your students know more than they can show you?"

**Why this is a holy shit moment:** Every teacher in that room has ELL students. Every one of them has seen a kid who KNOWS the material but can't demonstrate it. You just showed them a way to unlock those students.

---

## Insight 6: "Teachers Had Lower AI Literacy Than Students"
**What you found (from Wooster sessions):** When you taught 11th graders, the teachers in the room were MORE impressed than the students. The teachers had lower AI literacy than their own students. Mrs. Harvard said you "knew much more info than the adults" and had "much more credibility than you thought."

**What teachers need to hear (don't say this directly, DEMONSTRATE it):** The gap isn't between students and AI. It's between students and their teachers' understanding of AI. Students are already ahead. The PD event exists to close that gap. You close it by DOING, not by lecturing about it.

**How to use this tomorrow:** Don't tell them teachers have low AI literacy. Instead, during the live demo, casually say: "When we did this with 4th graders, they picked it up in about 10 minutes. I bet you can do it in 5." Then watch 50 adults realize that if a 4th grader can do this, they have no excuse.

---

## What to Actually Give Them (Beyond Prompts)

### 1. The Universal Template (Your Actual Curriculum Framework)
Give them the one-page framework you use for EVERY lesson:
1. Think First (student thinks before AI)
2. Engage with AI (structured, intentional use)
3. Evaluate & Reflect (judge, revise, explain)
4. Capture Learning (create an artifact as proof)

This isn't a prompt. This is a SYSTEM. They can apply it to any subject, any grade. Put this on the toolkit page.

### 2. The "3 Rules" Card
Every time a student (or teacher) uses AI:
1. **I think first** (What do I already know? What's my goal?)
2. **AI asks ME questions** (Not "give me the answer" — "ask me questions so you can help me better")
3. **I stay in control** (I evaluate, I decide, I revise)

This is simple enough to print, laminate, and put on every desk. Put this on the toolkit page.

### 3. A "First AI Lesson" They Can Teach Monday
Not a prompt. A 15-minute activity they can run with their actual students:
1. Ask students to draw a mind map of "What I think AI is" (3 min)
2. Share 4 facts: AI doesn't think, doesn't understand, doesn't know truth, recognizes patterns
3. Ask students: "What changed on your mind map?" (2 min)
4. Have students write ONE question about something they're curious about
5. Show them how question quality determines result quality (good prompt vs bad prompt)
6. Students try their question in ChatGPT and evaluate: "Did AI give me what I wanted? Why or why not?"

This is the first 15 minutes of your actual curriculum. You're giving them a tested, proven activity — not a theory.

title: Pd Pipeline Strategy
content:
# PD Day Pipeline Strategy — Client by 4/14

## The Reality

You have 12 days to land a project and a client. The PD event puts you in a room with 50+ educators and 3 decision-makers who already trust you. This is not a speaking gig. This is a sales pipeline disguised as a talk.

But you're not selling. You're listening. The Applied AI playbook says: **"Your first clients are not strangers. They are people who already know you, respect you, and would take your call."** Tomorrow, that room is full of those people.

---

## Who's In The Room (Ranked by Value)

### Tier 1: Decision-Makers (Can Sign a Check)

**Christopher Roche — Superintendent, Woodstock Public Schools**
- Already trusts you deeply (personal relationship since elementary school)
- Small K-8 district = faster decisions, less bureaucracy
- He ALREADY asked you for AI tool recommendations (Granola, meeting minutes, agendas)
- He mentioned convening a group of superintendents around AI
- **His pain:** He's a superintendent doing principal work too (subbing at elementary). He's drowning in admin tasks. Meeting minutes, agendas, email drafts, board prep.
- **The play:** After the event, say: *"Mr. Roche, you asked me about AI tools for meetings and email. What if instead of you figuring it out alone, I set up a system for you in 2 weeks? I'll build it around your actual workflow — meetings, agendas, follow-ups. If it doesn't save you 5 hours a week, you owe me nothing."*
- **Pilot price:** $2,000-3,000 for a 2-week sprint. He's a small district — price it accessible but not free.
- **Why this works:** He already asked for this. You're not pitching cold. You're answering his question with a concrete offer.

**Dr. Kristy Zaleta — Chief of Schools, Danbury Public Schools**
- Danbury is a MUCH bigger district than Woodstock
- She's already seen your pilot data
- She controls curriculum and instruction decisions
- **She already escalated you:** Invited you to spend a full day at DHS, introduced you to CTE (Elise Tobin Samaha), STEM (Dr. Nadeau), and Cybersecurity (Kris Davidson). That March 18 visit didn't happen — there is unfinished business.
- **Her pain:** Scaling AI professional development across a district. She can't do what you're doing at Woodstock for every school in Danbury.
- **The play:** Tomorrow, directly tell her: "I want to reschedule the DHS visit. And I have a proposal idea for a one-school AI pilot." She already championed you once — remind her why.
- **Pilot price:** $5,000 for a 4-week engagement at one school
- **Timeline:** DHS visit in late April → pilot proposal → May start

**Gina Williams — Director of Technology, Danbury**
- Controls tech procurement and adoption
- She sees the tools side, not just the instruction side
- **The play:** Learn what tools Danbury already uses. Ask her during the breakout: "What AI tools is the district currently evaluating?" Her answer tells you where the gaps are.

### Tier 2: Connectors (Introduce You to Clients)

**Mr. Roche → Other Superintendents**
- He already mentioned getting a group of superintendents together
- After tomorrow, nudge this: *"Mr. Roche, that group of superintendents you mentioned — I'd love to be part of that conversation. Even just listening would be valuable."*
- One superintendent in that group who has budget = your next client

**Teachers Who Light Up During Demos**
- Watch for the teacher who leans forward during the live demos
- Watch for whoever asks the best question
- These teachers talk to their principals. Their principal talks to their superintendent.
- Get their email. Follow up with: *"I noticed you were really engaged today. Would you be open to a 15-minute call? I'm building something for teachers and your perspective would be incredibly valuable."*

**Kristy Zaleta → Danbury Principals**
- She oversees all Danbury schools
- If she's impressed, she can put you in front of 10 principals

### Tier 3: Portfolio Builders (Build Your Case Study)

**One Teacher Volunteer**
- Find one teacher tomorrow who says "I want to try this"
- Offer: *"Let me set up your AI workflow for free this week. I'll check in with you in 5 days and document what happened."*
- This gives you a real teacher case study with before/after data
- Cost: 2-3 hours of your time. Value: proof that your system works for teachers, not just students.

---

## The Listening Framework

Applied AI says: **"Listen for pain."** Here are the exact signals to listen for tomorrow:

| What They Say | What It Means | Your Response |
|---------------|---------------|---------------|
| "We spend hours on [reports/grading/emails]" | High-volume repetitive task = AI automation opportunity | "How many hours per week? What does that process look like?" |
| "Our data is scattered everywhere" | Systems integration opportunity | "What tools are you using? Where does it break down?" |
| "I wish we could personalize more" | Differentiation at scale = exactly what AI does | "We built something for this. Want me to show you after?" |
| "The district wants us to use AI but nobody trained us" | PD gap = consulting opportunity | "That's exactly the gap we're filling. Who makes that decision at your district?" |
| "I don't have time to learn one more tool" | They need someone to set it up FOR them, not teach them | "What if someone just built it into your existing workflow?" |

**The anti-pitch (memorize this):**

> "I've been building AI systems for educators and I think I could save you real time on [the specific thing they mentioned]. Want me to put together a quick proposal? No pressure at all."

That's it. No deck. No pricing. Just a genuine offer based on a pain point they told you about.

---

## The 4/14 Path: Most Likely Scenarios

### Path A: Roche Pilot (Highest Probability — Go Here First)
**Why:** He already trusts you. He already asked for help with AI tools. Small district = fast decision.

**The killer angle:** You built Forge (app.forgelegatus.org) for yourself — CRM, time tracking, calendar, AI-powered email drafting, all in one system. Show him: *"Mr. Roche, this is what I built for my own workflow. I want to build something like this for yours."* He sees a working product, not a pitch deck. That's concrete.

| Date | Action |
|------|--------|
| April 3 | Deliver the PD event. After, pull Roche aside. Show him Forge on your phone. Say: "Remember when you asked about AI tools for meetings and emails? I built this for myself. I want to build your version." |
| April 4-5 | Send thank-you email. Include one line: "I've been thinking about the system I showed you. I have an idea for how to set that up for you in 2 weeks. Can I send you a quick proposal?" |
| April 6-7 | Send a 1-paragraph pilot proposal (see template below) |
| April 8-10 | He says yes. You start building. |
| April 14 | You have a client, a project in progress, and a case study forming. |

### Path B: Zaleta/Danbury (Bigger Play — Already Warm)
**Why:** She ALREADY escalated you. She invited you to spend a full day at DHS, introduced you to CTE and STEM leadership, and that visit never happened. There is unfinished business here. She's a champion waiting to be activated.

**Key contacts she opened for you (all in CRM):**
- Elise Tobin Samaha — CTE Dept Head, DHS (tobine@danbury.k12.ct.us)
- Dr. Nadeau — K-12 STEM Curriculum Administrator
- Kris Davidson — Director, Cybersecurity Academy
- Gina Williams — Director of Technology

**The play:** Tomorrow during the breakout, have a direct conversation with Zaleta. Say: *"Dr. Zaleta, I know the March 18 visit didn't work out. I'd love to reschedule — and I have some new ideas based on what we're seeing in the libraries. Can we get that on the calendar this month?"*

Then pivot to the real ask: *"I've also been thinking — what if instead of just speaking to students, we ran a 4-week AI pilot at one Danbury school? I'd handle the curriculum and training. You'd get measurable data. That's something I could put together a proposal for."*

| Date | Action |
|------|--------|
| April 3 | Have the conversation with Zaleta. Reschedule DHS visit. Plant pilot seed. |
| April 5-7 | Follow-up email: "Great seeing you Friday. Here are two things: (1) let's get the DHS visit on the calendar, (2) I'd love to send you a brief proposal for a one-school pilot." |
| April 8-14 | Reschedule DHS visit for late April. Send pilot proposal ($5,000, 4 weeks). |
| Late April | DHS visit → builds more trust → pilot decision. |

### Path C: Teacher Case Study → Referral
**Why:** Lower barrier. Free work, but generates proof fast.

| Date | Action |
|------|--------|
| April 3 | Identify 1 enthusiastic teacher. Offer to set up their AI workflow free. |
| April 4-7 | Build their workflow (2-3 hours). Check in after 3 days. |
| April 8-10 | Document results. Ask: "Would you be comfortable sharing this with your principal?" |
| April 10-14 | Principal conversation → potential district engagement. |

**Run all three paths simultaneously.** Path A is your best shot for 4/14. Path B is your biggest long-term play.

---

## The Pilot Proposal Template

When you're ready to pitch Roche (or anyone), send this via email. Keep it short.

---

> **Subject:** quick idea for your meetings
>
> Mr. Roche,
>
> Thank you again for Friday. The teachers were incredible.
>
> I've been thinking about what you asked me back in March — the AI tools for meeting minutes, agendas, and email drafts. I have an idea.
>
> **The problem:** You're running a district, subbing as principal, and spending hours on admin tasks that AI could handle.
>
> **What I'd build:** A custom AI workflow tailored to your actual meetings and communication. It would:
> - Auto-generate meeting minutes and action items from your notes
> - Draft the following week's agenda based on open items
> - Draft email responses for your review (you edit and send)
>
> **Timeline:** 2 weeks. I build it around your real workflow, not a generic tool.
>
> **Investment:** $2,500. If it doesn't save you at least 5 hours a week, I'll refund every penny.
>
> Would you be open to a quick call this week to walk through it?
>
> All the best,
> Brayan

---

## Pricing Reference (From Applied AI Playbook)

| What You're Offering | Price Range | Notes |
|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| AI workflow audit (discovery) | $2,000 | One-time assessment of where AI fits |
| 2-week sprint pilot | $2,500-5,000 | Single problem, concrete deliverable |
| 4-week school pilot (PD + build) | $5,000-8,000 | Training + system build |
| Monthly retainer (ongoing support) | $1,000-2,000/mo | Monitoring, optimization, coaching |

**For Roche specifically:** $2,500 is accessible for a small district. Frame it as 10-20% of the value. If the system saves him 5 hours/week at his loaded rate (~$75/hr), that's $19,500/year in recovered time. $2,500 is 13% of year-one value.

**Do NOT go free.** The Applied AI playbook is explicit: *"Underpricing devalues your expertise."* Even if it's your first paid engagement, charge something. It changes the dynamic from "favor" to "professional engagement."

---

## Mental Checklist: During Every Conversation Tomorrow

1. **Listen first.** Do not pitch. Ask questions.
2. **Identify one pain point.** What takes them the most time? What frustrates them?
3. **Quantify it.** "How many hours a week does that take you?" (This is the number you use later in your proposal.)
4. **Plant the seed.** "I build AI systems that solve exactly that. Would you be open to hearing a quick idea?"
5. **Get their contact.** Email or phone. Not a business card you'll lose.
6. **Log it immediately.** Open your phone, add them to the CRM app right there.

---

## The One Thing

You are not giving a talk tomorrow. You are walking into a room where 50 people are about to watch you demonstrate that you can solve their problems. Every conversation after the keynote is a pipeline opportunity.

The goal is not 50 leads. The goal is **one person who says "can you do that for me?"** Find that person. That's your client by 4/14.

title: Roche Pipeline
content:
# The Roche Pipeline — Step by Step

## The Applied AI Framework (Adapted for Education)

The playbook says: Trust → Anti-Pitch → Pilot → Deliver → Referral Flywheel. Here's how that maps to Roche, starting tomorrow.

---

## Stage 1: TRUST (Already Done)

**What the playbook says:** "Your first clients are not strangers. They are people who already know you, respect you, and would take your call."

**Where you are with Roche:** You're past this. He played football with you after school. He called his colleague the day you emailed him. He invited you to keynote his PD day. He introduced you to Zaleta, Williams, and Mervis. He offered to rearrange his schedule for you. Trust is built.

**Status: Complete.**

---

## Stage 2: DEMONSTRATE VALUE (Tomorrow — April 3)

**What the playbook says:** "Listen for pain signals. Identify one concrete pain point that's measurable and urgent."

**What you do tomorrow:**

**9:00-9:30 AM — Keynote**
Deliver the presentation. 4 live demos. Toolkit QR code. You are establishing yourself as the AI expert in the room. Roche is watching. The entire audience is social proof for him — if 50 teachers respond to you, his confidence in you compounds.

**9:30 AM-2:30 PM — Breakout Support**
Float between sessions. Listen. Collect data. Have conversations. But keep one eye on Roche throughout the day. Note what he reacts to. Note what questions he asks. Note what excites him.

**End of Day — The Private Conversation**
Find Roche after the event winds down. This is not a pitch. This is a conversation between two people who know each other.

Say:

> "Mr. Roche, you asked me back in March about AI tools for your meetings and emails. I've been thinking about it a lot. I didn't want to just recommend another app. What I've been building is something different — it's a personal AI system that learns how you work and helps you think, not just automate. I built one for myself and it's changed how I operate. I'd love to show you what yours could look like. Can I come back and walk you through it?"

**What you're doing here:** You're not pitching. You're referencing a conversation HE started. You're offering to SHOW, not sell. The playbook calls this the anti-pitch: "No deck. No case study. No pricing sheet. Just a human being offering to help."

**His likely response:** Yes. He always says yes to you.

**Status: Tomorrow.**

---

## Stage 3: SHOW THE JARVIS (April 7-10)

**What the playbook says:** "Move to the pilot conversation only after trust is established and pain is identified."

**What you do:** Schedule a 1-hour meeting at Woodstock. Bring your laptop. Show him YOUR Jarvis — how you use it, what it does, how it thinks. Then show him a mockup of what HIS could look like.

**The meeting flow (1 hour):**

**First 15 min — Show yours:**
Open your my-jarvis workspace. Show him:
- "This is my people directory. Everyone I've talked to, with full context."
- "This is how I process meetings. I brain dump notes, and it generates minutes with action items."
- "This is how I draft emails. It knows my voice."
- "This is my calendar and time tracker. All connected."

Let him react. Let him ask questions. Let him see what's possible.

**Next 15 min — Interview him:**
Ask him about HIS workflow. Not to pitch — to understand.
- "Walk me through a typical Monday. What hits your inbox first?"
- "When you leave a meeting, what happens to those notes?"
- "What's the thing that takes you the most time that you wish you could hand off?"
- "When you're prepping for a board meeting, where does that information live?"

Write his answers down. These become the blueprint for his Jarvis.

**Next 15 min — Show him what his could look like:**
Pull up a blank workspace structure. Walk through it:
- "This would be your people directory — board members, principals, teachers."
- "This is where your meeting notes would go. AI turns them into minutes and action items."
- "These are skills — recipes for things you do repeatedly. Agenda building, email drafting, board prep."
- "And anything I build for myself that fits your workflow, I push to your system."

**Last 15 min — Let him talk:**
Don't sell. Let him process. He'll either ask "how do we do this?" or "what would this cost?" or "when can we start?"

If he asks about cost, say: "Let me put together a plan based on what you told me today. I want to make sure it's built around your actual workflow, not a generic template."

If he doesn't ask about cost, say: "I'd love to build this for you. Let me put a plan together and send it over this week."

**Status: Schedule this before you leave Woodstock on April 3.**

---

## Stage 4: THE PROPOSAL (April 10-11)

**What the playbook says:** "I'll build [specific deliverable] that [specific outcome]. Timeline: [2-4 weeks]. If it doesn't deliver the value we discussed, we'll figure out how to make it right."

**What you send:** A short email. Not a PDF. Not a slide deck. An email.

The proposal should reference HIS words from the interview. Use the specific things he told you.

Structure:
1. **One sentence on the problem** — reference what he said about his pain
2. **What you'll build** — his personal Jarvis, configured for his workflow
3. **What's included** — setup, training, his first set of skills (meeting minutes, agendas, email drafts, board prep)
4. **Timeline** — 2 weeks from start to self-sufficient
5. **What happens after** — you stay available for new tools and support
6. **The assurance** — "If this doesn't change how you work, we'll make it right."

**Keep it under 200 words.** The playbook says: "Don't over-produce this."

**Status: Send within 48 hours of the show-and-tell meeting.**

---

## Stage 5: BUILD AND DELIVER (April 14-28)

**What the playbook says:** "One problem. One deliverable. Easy to say yes."

**What you actually do:**

**Week 1:**
- Install Claude Code on his laptop (or set up the GitHub repo)
- Build his USER.md together (30-min session, in person or Zoom)
- Configure his skills: meeting-minutes, agenda-builder, email-drafter
- Populate his people directory with key contacts (board, principals)
- Training session 1: how to use it daily

**Week 2:**
- Check in: what worked, what didn't, what confused him
- Add board-prep skill based on his actual board meeting format
- Push any tools from your own system that fit (calendar, time tracker)
- Training session 2: deeper capabilities, people files, artifacts
- Get his feedback: "What else would you want this to do?"

**End of Week 2:** He's self-sufficient. He's using it daily. He's telling people about it.

**Status: This is the work.**

---

## Stage 6: THE REFERRAL FLYWHEEL (May onward)

**What the playbook says:** "Happy clients are your marketing department. Results generate referrals."

**What happens with Roche:**
- He uses his Jarvis every day
- He mentions it to other superintendents (he already wanted to convene a group about AI)
- One of them says "can I get that?"
- Roche says "talk to Brayan"
- You set up superintendent #2 using 80% of the same skills library
- Repeat

**The flywheel:**
```
Roche uses Jarvis daily
    → Tells superintendent friends
        → They want one too
            → You build theirs faster (shared skills)
                → They tell THEIR network
                    → You become THE person for AI in education leadership
```

**What the playbook calls this:** "Expansion: Organizations have dozens of problems; solving one earns you the right to solve more. Word-of-mouth: Happy clients become your marketing without budget requirements."

---

## The Full Timeline

| Date | Stage | Action |
|------|-------|--------|
| **April 3** | Demonstrate | Keynote + private conversation with Roche. Ask to come back and show him. |
| **April 4** | Follow-up | Thank-you email to Roche. "I'll send you a few times to come show you what I'm thinking." |
| **April 7-10** | Show | 1-hour meeting at Woodstock. Show your Jarvis. Interview him. Show what his looks like. |
| **April 10-11** | Propose | Short email proposal based on his words from the interview. |
| **April 12-13** | Confirm | He says yes. Set a start date. |
| **April 14-28** | Build | 2-week build. Setup, training, skills, tools. |
| **End of April** | Deliver | He's self-sufficient. Using it daily. |
| **May** | Flywheel | He introduces you to other superintendents. You build their Jarvises. |

---

## What NOT to Do

- **Don't pitch at the PD event.** Demonstrate. That's it.
- **Don't quote a price until after the show-and-tell.** You need to hear HIS words about HIS pain before you can price it properly.
- **Don't send a PDF proposal.** A short email works. The playbook says: "Don't over-produce this."
- **Don't skip the interview.** His Jarvis must be built on HIS workflow, not your assumptions.
- **Don't underprice.** The playbook is explicit: "Clients who won't pay a reasonable amount for a 2-week pilot are not clients you want."
- **Don't give it away for free.** Even for Roche. Charging changes the dynamic from "favor" to "professional engagement." He will respect it more.

title: Woodstock Pd Battle Plan
content:
# PD Day Battle Plan — April 3, 2026

## The Mission

Walk into Woodstock Middle School and make 50 educators believe that AI is their ally, not their enemy. Leave them with a tool they can use Monday morning. Leave Mr. Roche seeing you as the person who can transform his workflow. Leave as the person everyone in that room wants to talk to about AI.

---

## 3 Goals (In Order of Priority)

**1. Shift the room from fear to curiosity.**
These are teachers. Most have heard "AI is coming" and felt dread. Your job is to make them feel excited — not by hype, but by showing them something useful in real-time. If they leave thinking "I could actually try that," you won.

**2. Deliver a takeaway they'll use after today.**
The Teacher AI Toolkit at `nextgenerationlearners.com/woodstock`. 3 Rules, a 15-minute lesson they can run Monday, and 6 copy-paste drivers. This is what separates you from every other AI speaker — you don't just talk, you hand them the keys.

**3. Plant the seed with Roche.**
After the event, the private conversation. "I built a personal AI system for myself. I'd love to show you what yours could look like." That's the pipeline. Everything else supports this moment.

---

## Tonight: Prep Checklist

| What | Done? |
|------|-------|
| Run through keynote out loud 2x (time it — under 28 min) | [ ] |
| Open ChatGPT, run all 4 demos, screenshot each as Wi-Fi backup | [ ] |
| Type out the 4 demo prompts in a Notes doc — ready to paste | [ ] |
| Charge laptop + phone, pack HDMI cable + USB-C adapter | [ ] |
| Pick outfit — blazer or button-down, no tie, clean sneakers | [ ] |
| Set alarm for 6:00 AM | [ ] |

---

## Tomorrow Timeline

| Time | What |
|------|------|
| 6:00 AM | Wake up. Shower. Eat. Pray. |
| 7:00 AM | Leave for Woodstock (~1hr drive, arrive 8:00) |
| 8:00 AM | Arrive at Woodstock Middle School. Find Mr. Roche. |
| 8:00-8:30 | **Setup:** Connect laptop to smart TV. Test screen, sound, Wi-Fi. Open ChatGPT and test it. Have demo prompts in a separate tab. |
| 8:30-8:50 | **Meet Kristy and Gina.** Introduce yourself. Ask what they're covering so you don't overlap. Ask how you can support their sessions. |
| 8:50-9:00 | **Stand at the front. Water bottle. Deep breath.** |
| 9:00-9:30 | **KEYNOTE** |
| 9:30-9:35 | Transition. Show QR code for toolkit. Let people scan. |
| 9:35-~11:00 | **Breakout sessions.** Float between Gina's and Kristy's rooms. |
| 11:00-2:30 | Stay as long as useful. 1-on-1 conversations with interested teachers. Get emails. |
| End of day | **Private conversation with Roche.** The seed. |

---

## The Keynote: 30 Minutes

The structure is: **WHO → WHY → WHAT I SAW → WHAT WE DID → WHAT YOU CAN DO → TOOLS**

You lead with your story. Your story leads into the fear. The fear leads into your why. Your why earns you the right to teach them. Then you teach them.

---

### Part 1: Who I Am (3 min)

**No slide needed. Just you talking.**

> "Good morning. My name is Brayan Tenesaca. I grew up in Danbury. I went to AIS Magnet School — Mr. Roche was actually my principal."

(Pause. Let the room react. Roche will smile. The room connects you to someone they know.)

> "I played soccer all four years of high school. Won five trophies. Scored over 100 goals. I now train mixed martial arts at Babson College, where I'm a freshman."

(This matters. You're not a "tech kid." You're an athlete, a competitor, a grinder. They need to see that before you talk about AI.)

> "I'm also the co-founder of Next Generation Learners. We run AI literacy programs for students in libraries and schools across Connecticut — Danbury, Ridgefield, Easton, and now here in Woodstock. I'm 18 years old."

(Let that land. 18. Founded a company. Running programs. In their building.)

---

### Part 2: The Fear — Why I'm Here (4 min)

**This is the emotional core of your entire talk. Slow down. Mean every word.**

> "When I got to Babson, I saw something that scared me. I saw students my age building entire businesses with AI — real revenue, real products — while other students were terrified to even open it. The gap was massive. And I realized: that gap doesn't start in college. It starts now. It starts in your classrooms."

> "So I started paying attention. And I saw fear everywhere."

> "I saw the fear of parents — worried their kids will never get exposed to AI, or worried they'll get TOO exposed to it without guidance."

> "I saw the fear of students — afraid they'll get caught using AI, not knowing there's a right way to use it."

> "I saw the fear of schools — that they won't adapt fast enough, that the world is moving and they're standing still."

> "And I saw the fear of teachers."

(Pause here. Look at them.)

> "I saw teachers afraid that AI will replace them. That their job — the job they pour their heart into — is somehow disposable. That a machine can do what they do."

(Pause again.)

> "And I thought: I have to do something about this. Because that fear? It's wrong. And I need you to hear me on this."

---

### Part 3: Why Teachers Matter (3 min)

**This is where you earn the room. Every teacher in that cafeteria needs to hear this.**

> "AI is not going to replace you. It will never replace you. Here's what it WILL do: it will replace people who refuse to learn how to use it. That's different. That's a choice, not a fate."

> "But let me tell you what AI can never do."

> "AI cannot care about your students. You can tell a machine what to do. You can tell it how to respond. But you cannot teach it to care. It doesn't stay after school because it noticed a kid was having a bad day. It doesn't throw a football with a 5th grader because that kid needed someone to show up for him."

(This is about Roche. He'll know. The room will feel it.)

> "No one can take away your teaching style. No one can take away the way you connect with a student who's about to give up. No one can replicate the moment a kid's eyes light up because YOU explained it in the way that finally made sense."

> "Teachers are often overlooked. People talk about your job like it's replaceable. But teaching is one of the most important jobs on earth. Our first teachers were our parents. Then you. You are the ones who pass wisdom from one generation to the next. You fuel the next generation. Without you, we don't have a society."

> "So no — AI is not here to replace you. AI is here to make the most important job in the world a little easier. So you can spend less time on the admin, and more time doing what only YOU can do: caring about your students."

(Let the room breathe. Don't rush into the next part.)

---

### Part 4: What We Found (3 min)

> "So we went and tested this. We ran a 4-session AI literacy program for 4th and 5th graders at the Danbury Library. And what we found surprised even us."

> "Students showed up thinking AI was magic — that it knows everything, that it's a brain. By session 4, those same kids were disagreeing with AI. They were revising their own prompts. They were explaining WHY they agreed or disagreed with the output."

> "Here's the insight that changed everything for us: AI literacy doesn't replace thinking. It makes thinking VISIBLE. For the first time, we could actually see what was happening inside a student's head — through their prompts, their revisions, their reflections."

> "And one more thing: the students who asked the worst questions learned the most. Because they saw the gap between a vague question and a specific one in real time. And that skill — asking better questions — transfers to everything. Not just AI. Life."

---

### Part 5: The Mindset — Partner vs Employee (2 min)

> "Here's the frame we teach every student, and it's the same one I want to leave you with today."

> "There are two ways to use AI. As an employee: 'Do this for me.' One prompt, one answer, done. That's what students do when nobody teaches them. That's what cheating looks like."

> "Or as a partner: 'Help me think.' Back-and-forth. Questions. Iteration. The human stays in control."

> "When we taught 4th graders this distinction, something clicked. They stopped asking AI for answers and started asking AI for better questions. That's the shift."

---

### Part 6: Live Demos — What This Looks Like For You (10 min)

> "Enough talking. Let me show you."

(Switch to ChatGPT. Run all 4 demos live.)

**Demo 1 — Lesson Plan Partner (3 min)**
```
I teach 6th grade ELA. I'm starting a unit on persuasive writing next week. Before you give me a lesson plan, ask me 5 questions about my students so you can personalize it.
```
SAY: "Notice I didn't ask for a lesson plan. I asked it to ask ME questions first. That's the partner mindset. Your expertise leads. AI supports."

**Demo 2 — Differentiation in 60 Seconds (3 min)**
```
Now adapt this for a student who understands the concepts but struggles with English as a second language. Keep the thinking demands high but simplify the language.
```
Then:
```
Now create a version for a student who finishes early and needs extension.
```
SAY: "60 seconds. Two differentiated versions. You made every decision. How many of your students know more than they can show you because of a language barrier? AI just removed that barrier. Not the rigor — the barrier."

**Demo 3 — Student Simulator (2 min)**
```
Act as a 7th grader who is confused about fractions. I'm going to explain it to you, and you tell me where my explanation is unclear or where a real student might get lost.
```
SAY: "Practice your lesson before class, not during it. Find the gaps before your students do."

**Demo 4 — Feedback Writer (2 min)**
```
Here's a paragraph a 5th grader wrote about their favorite animal: "Dogs are the best pets because they are fun and nice. They play with you and love you. I want a golden retriever." Give feedback the way a great teacher would — specific, encouraging, focused on one thing to improve. Don't rewrite it.
```
SAY: "Specific. Encouraging. One clear next step. And you decide if it sounds like YOU before it ever reaches the student. That's the partner mindset."

---

### Part 7: The Toolkit (2 min)

> "Everything I just showed you is yours. I built a toolkit with 6 of these AI drivers — copy, paste into ChatGPT, and use them today."

> "There's also a 15-minute AI lesson you can run with your students on Monday. Tested with real kids. No prep needed."

> "And 3 rules you can print and put on every desk. Rule 1: I think first. Rule 2: AI asks ME questions. Rule 3: I stay in control."

(Show QR code for `nextgenerationlearners.com/woodstock`)

> "Scan this. Everything is there. It's free. It's yours."

---

### Part 8: Close (2 min)

> "I started this because I saw fear. Fear from every direction. And I realized that fear doesn't go away by avoiding the thing you're afraid of. It goes away when someone shows you how to use it."

> "You are that someone for your students. You are the ones who will teach them to think with AI, not outsource their thinking to it. Nobody else is going to do that. Not an app. Not a YouTube video. You."

> "Your students are watching. Show them what's possible."

> "Gina and Kristy are going to take you deeper in the breakout sessions. I'll be there too. Come find me. I want to hear your questions, your concerns, and your ideas. Thank you."

**Total: ~29 min. 1 min buffer.**

---

### The 4 Live Demos (Have These in a Notes Doc Ready to Paste)

**Demo 1 — Lesson Plan Partner (3 min)**
```
I teach 6th grade ELA. I'm starting a unit on persuasive writing next week. Before you give me a lesson plan, ask me 5 questions about my students so you can personalize it.
```
SAY: "Notice I didn't ask for a lesson plan. I asked it to ask ME questions. That's the partner mindset."

**Demo 2 — Differentiation in 60 Seconds (3 min)**
```
Now adapt this for a student who understands the concepts but struggles with English as a second language. Keep the thinking demands high but simplify the language.
```
Then:
```
Now create a version for a student who finishes early and needs extension.
```
SAY: "60 seconds. Two differentiated versions. And you made every decision. How many of your students know more than they can show you because of a language barrier? AI just removed that barrier."

**Demo 3 — Student Simulator (2 min)**
```
Act as a 7th grader who is confused about fractions. I'm going to explain it to you, and you tell me where my explanation is unclear or where a real student might get lost.
```
SAY: "Practice your lesson before class, not during it. Find the gaps now."

**Demo 4 — Feedback Writer (2 min)**
```
Here's a paragraph a 5th grader wrote about their favorite animal: "Dogs are the best pets because they are fun and nice. They play with you and love you. I want a golden retriever." Give feedback the way a great teacher would — specific, encouraging, focused on one thing to improve. Don't rewrite it.
```
SAY: "Specific. Encouraging. One thing to improve. And you decide if it sounds like you before it reaches the student."

---

## The 6 Holy Shit Insights (Weave Into Your Talk)

These are from your actual pilot data. No other PD session has this.

1. **The Ego Barrier:** Teachers' biggest blocker isn't skill — it's ego. Reframe: "Your 20 years PLUS AI makes you unstoppable."
2. **Students already know more than teachers think.** 4th graders were disagreeing with AI by session 4.
3. **AI makes thinking VISIBLE.** Before/after mind maps showed growth worksheets never capture.
4. **Question quality = life quality.** Teaching better questions of AI taught better questions of everything.
5. **The ELL breakthrough.** AI separates concept mastery from language proficiency.
6. **Teachers had lower AI literacy than students.** Don't say this — demonstrate it. "4th graders picked this up in 10 minutes. I bet you can do it in 5."

---

## What Roche Asked You To Cover (Checklist)

From his March 25 email:

- [ ] The fear many have of embracing AI and why this needs to change → Slide 3
- [ ] How to get started with AI as a teacher → Live demos + toolkit QR
- [ ] Your success story, how you got started → Slide 2
- [ ] Programs you've developed with NGL → Mention Danbury, Ridgefield, Easton in Slide 2
- [ ] Anything else → Partner vs Employee + toolkit

---

## The Private Conversation with Roche (After the Event)

Find him when things wind down. Say:

> "Mr. Roche, you asked me back in March about AI tools for your meetings and emails. I've been thinking about it a lot. I didn't want to just recommend another app. What I've been building is something different — it's a personal AI system that learns how you work and helps you think, not just automate. I built one for myself and it's changed how I operate. I'd love to show you what yours could look like. Can I come back and walk you through it?"

No price. No proposal. Just: "Can I show you?"

---

## If Things Go Wrong

| Problem | Solution |
|---------|----------|
| Wi-Fi dies | Screenshots saved on laptop. Story and framing still land. |
| Smart TV won't connect | Present from laptop, walk around showing it close. |
| Audience is quiet | Direct question: "You teach math, right? What would you ask AI?" |
| Run over time | Cut Demo 4. First 3 are enough. |
| Run under time | Open the floor: "What's the one thing you're most worried about?" |
| Nerves | Roche believes in you. You taught 4th graders. Adults are easier. |

---

## The One Thing

You are not giving a talk tomorrow. You are walking into a room where 50 people are about to watch you prove that AI can make them better at what they already do. Every conversation after the keynote is a pipeline opportunity. The goal is one person who says "can you do that for me?" Find that person.

title: Woodstock Pd Debrief
content:
# Woodstock PD Debrief — April 3, 2026

## The Bottom Line

Today changed the trajectory of NGL. We walked in to give a PD talk. We walked out with a validated business model, a pipeline to Danbury Public Schools, a clear product vision (sovereign operating system for educators), and the exact budget category to sell into (professional learning). Everything below is the raw intelligence organized for action.

---

## 1. The Product Vision: Sovereign Operating System

**What clicked today:** Every pain point — email overload, disconnected apps, privacy fears, language barriers — traces back to one root cause: educators don't have a sovereign system that works for them.

**The pitch (crystallized from the Roche conversation):**
- Local data storage — nothing trains AI models
- All-in-one system — replaces the app chaos teachers deal with
- Customizable per district, per school, per teacher
- Claude is the brain, Jarvis is the body
- If Claude disappears tomorrow, swap in another AI model — sovereignty means no vendor lock-in

**What teachers saw today that made it real:**
- Built a financial literacy app in 5 minutes using voice prompts
- Updated the live website in real-time during the session
- Created a QR code for email capture while Gina was presenting
- Added multiplication game on request from a 4th grade teacher — she watched it deploy live
- Students at Danbury Library built working websites with custom products in one hour

**Key quote from Roche:** "I definitely want to look at what can be done selfishly for me, from my perspective of leadership. How can this make my world easier?"

---

## 2. The Killer Use Case: Language + Content Simultaneously

**This is the 80/20.** Roche said it multiple times. The language barrier problem in diverse districts is the single biggest unsolved pain point.

**The problem:**
- Danbury has Spanish, Portuguese, Russian, Arabic, and many more languages
- Students sit in class waiting for translation, falling behind in content
- A brilliant Portuguese-speaking 3rd grader waits for his friend to finish work so that friend can translate for him — wasting both kids' time
- Current tools (I-Ready/My Path) only support Spanish — nothing else
- Dual language programs work but funding dries up after kindergarten — no pipeline into 1st grade and beyond
- Teachers are not bilingual — "Danbury doesn't have enough Spanish teachers"

**The opportunity Roche described:**
- A system that delivers content AND language acquisition simultaneously
- "Instead of two discrete skills, you could do both at the same time"
- Student hears the math concept in their native language 3-4 times (per research) while learning English
- Shows mastery by grade level — "Can I identify what a fraction is? Shade 2/6 of this rectangle?" — then moves up
- "If you could do that in such a way that hits content standards with language support... you'd be a very wealthy individual"

**Why Danbury is the perfect case study:** Most diverse district in CT, every language barrier problem exists there at scale. Solve it there, solve it everywhere.

---

## 3. The Pipeline: Danbury Public Schools

**The play (directly from Roche):**

1. Contact **Dr. Casimiro** (Superintendent, Danbury Public Schools)
2. Use the word **"academy"** repeatedly — "Use the phrase academy six times in your first three sentences"
3. Say: "I'm from Danbury schools and this is what I'm doing"
4. Mention that **Christy Zita** told you to use the word "academy" — "she'll believe you"
5. Position under **professional learning budgets** — this is where the money already sits

**Budget alignment strategy (from Roche):**
- Falls under professional learning OR technology budget
- "It's maximizing your time, teaching you how to be more effective — all of those things help you be more effective at your job"
- Could also fall under tech, but professional learning is the strongest fit
- The school pays, not the individual — "My goal isn't to have the user pay for it. My goal is to have the school pay for it because that increases adoption"

**Matt Burns (Worcester School) — already warm:**
- Already expressed interest in personal productivity system setup
- Use him as a reference when contacting Casimiro
- He's the bridge into the Danbury network

---

## 4. Educator Pain Points (Validated Today)

### Administrative Burden
- Superintendents and teachers spend **60% of time on admin** vs. classroom
- Roche: "I'll literally get 500 emails a day" in a small district
- "I'm trying to do many jobs at once... one person for finance, phenomenal. After that, nothing."
- He works 7:15 AM to 7-9 PM regularly, missing his son's weekday games
- Email categorization, prioritization, automated responses — immediate wins

### Disconnected App Chaos
- "Multiple disconnected apps creating workflow problems"
- "Teachers go out because of frustration — they go into the wild wild west and find apps that they're not authorized to use"
- Gina (Danbury tech director): "What we're trying to do is build a teacher toolbox... basic apps that everybody should be using"
- **This validates the all-in-one sovereign system approach**

### Privacy & Data Concerns
- Teachers are concerned about AI training on their data
- Art teachers worried about AI replacing creativity
- Students shaming teachers for AI energy consumption
- The sovereign/local model directly addresses the #1 fear

### Math Education Pipeline Failure
- Students arrive at middle school without fraction understanding
- Gap between conceptual learning and algorithmic proficiency
- "Why don't 6th graders know what a fraction is?" — because it wasn't taught properly in earlier grades
- Multiplication facts mastery is critical — 4th grade teacher confirmed this live
- Too much time reviewing, not enough advancing

### Regional Service Centers Are Useless
- Roche: "I wouldn't pay them $5... it's the company line. Somebody out of work. They're not doing what they should."
- "People that heard about it, studied it, but are not doing anything with it are not the best at presenting about it because they lack the real experience"
- **This is NGL's competitive advantage — we're practitioners, not theorists**

---

## 5. What Worked Today (Proof Points)

### The Keynote
- Teachers did a "whip around" at the end — every single person had something they were going to do Monday
- Kindergarten teachers: "I'm going to use it as a thought partner to brainstorm"
- Multiple people said: "You're a real person" — the authenticity landed
- Assistant principal told Gina after: "He's so real and down to earth... sometimes people come with pie in the sky ideas. He's an actual real person."

### Live Building
- Financial literacy game built in 5 minutes — teachers watched it deploy
- Multiplication game requested by 4th grade teacher (Kendra) — built and deployed on the spot
- QR code for email capture created during Gina's session
- Website updated in real-time — teachers saw the Woodstock toolkit page go live
- "That's crazy" — Kendra's reaction watching the multiplication game deploy

### The Art Teacher Conversation (Hallway)
- Concern: student says "AI will do everything for me in the arts"
- Response: "Have you ever thought of hanging an AI painting on your wall? No. It can create, but it cannot pull that emotion, that heart, that feeling."
- Reframe: student can use AI as starting point for animation — "She can run her own animation. She can start producing."

### The Environmental Concern Conversation (Hallway)
- Parent's kids shame her for AI energy use
- Response: "Nothing is perfect. Trade-offs exist with everything — internet, phone. Entrepreneurs see that problem and want to make it better. It will get solved because people like your kids care about the environment."
- "This is here to stay. What's your idea on solving this?"

---

## 6. Breakthrough Coach Comparison

Roche referenced **The Breakthrough Coach** — a popular education leadership program:
- Based in Florida, tours globally (Australia, US)
- Premise: "You're spending 80 hours doing a 40-hour job. Here's why."
- Problem: it's paper-based, 1980s methodology, delegates work to secretaries
- "Everything that's not within the leadership realm, you don't do — giving everybody else the work"
- **NGL/Jarvis is the modern, AI-powered version of this**
- "This is a more contemporary solution to what that is"
- Roche wants to see it applied to his own workflow

---

## 7. Specific Asks & Warm Leads

| Person | Role | Ask | Priority |
|--------|------|-----|----------|
| Matt Roche | Superintendent, Woodstock | Personal Jarvis setup, follow-up meeting | HIGH |
| Matt Burns | Head of Worcester School | Personal productivity system demo | HIGH |
| Dr. Casimiro | Superintendent, Danbury | Cold outreach using "academy" framing | HIGH |
| Gina Williams | Tech Director, Danbury | Report card comment generator | MEDIUM |
| Kendra (4th grade teacher) | Woodstock Elementary | Multiplication practice tools (DONE) | DONE |
| 3rd grade teacher | Woodstock Elementary | Financial literacy game (DONE) | DONE |
| Danbury district | Multiple | Centralized teacher toolkit | FUTURE |

---

## 8. Business Model Insights

### Pricing Strategy
- Align with **professional learning budgets** — school pays, not individual
- Position as alternative to existing spend: "You're spending money there. This is better because it has these features."
- One-day intensive workshop model for administrators — setup + training
- Ongoing: anyone from NGL team can push tools to client's Jarvis remotely

### Competitive Positioning
- vs. Regional Service Centers: "They lack real experience. We're practitioners."
- vs. Breakthrough Coach: "Contemporary solution" — AI-powered, not paper-based
- vs. Harvard AI speakers: "Show me it in practice. I don't care about theory."
- vs. ChatGPT/generic AI: Sovereign, local, doesn't train on your data, builds actual tools

### Sales Motion
1. Trust first — be in the building, talk to people, don't sell anything
2. Demonstrate live — build something they need in front of them
3. Let them experience it — voice typing, seeing deployment in real-time
4. "Can I come back and show you what yours could look like?"
5. Align to existing budget category — professional learning
6. School pays, not individual — increases adoption

### The "Sell Before You Build" Principle
- "I don't build things that people don't need. I don't build things people won't buy."
- Strike a deal first, build with them, not for them
- "Combining domain experts and technical experts — being there in the classroom and building with the teachers themselves"

---

## 9. Market Intelligence

### Connecticut Education Landscape
- 169 school districts — massive inefficiency
- State contributes only ~20% of education funding; ~70-80% from local property taxes
- 5th highest per-pupil spending nationally, 48th in state contribution
- Small districts (some with 52 total K-8 students) struggle with resources
- Political resistance to regional consolidation
- ECS (Education Cost Share) hasn't increased since 2013

### Texas Opportunity
- Applied AI Society backer offering funding + Texas expansion
- Academy model is widespread in Texas — large, well-funded districts
- Dual language programs thriving (San Antonio example — bilingual by 5th grade)
- Superintendents paid well, more operational flexibility
- "This is going to be the Applied AI Society hub"
- Flying to Texas this summer

### The ELA Teacher Problem
- "The one group that has the biggest issue with AI is the ELA teachers"
- Immediately default to "kids will cheat"
- Danbury HS was exploring feedback apps — unclear where that went
- Woodstock Academy (private, across the street): "No, no cheating" — refusing to embrace
- Need case studies to shift this — show partner mindset in action

---

## 10. Next Steps (Priority Order)

### This Week
1. **Email Dr. Casimiro** — use academy framing, mention Matt Burns and Danbury connection
2. **Schedule follow-up with Roche** — one-day intensive workshop to set up his personal Jarvis
3. **Email Matt Burns** — schedule demo of full system setup
4. **Send thank-you to Gina and Christy** — they brought massive value today

### Next 2 Weeks
5. **Build report card comment generator** for Gina — quick win, deepens Danbury relationship
6. **Prepare one-day intensive workshop model** — pricing, deliverables, what's included
7. **Research professional learning budget categories** — know the exact line items
8. **Document the language + content use case** — this is the pitch deck centerpiece

### This Month
9. **Pilot proposal for Danbury** — language + content tool, positioned under professional learning
10. **Prepare Texas trip** — Applied AI Society backing, academy model research
11. **Build case study from today** — Woodstock PD, teacher reactions, live demos, outcomes

---

## 11. The Paradigm Shift

What changed today:

**Before:** NGL = AI literacy programs for kids in libraries and schools
**After:** NGL = Sovereign operating systems for educators and administrators that solve the language barrier, admin burden, and disconnected tooling problems in K-12 education

The library-to-school-to-district pipeline is validated. The product is the sovereign OS. The budget is professional learning. The beachhead is Danbury. The killer app is language + content simultaneously.

Trust → Demonstrate → Build With Them → Scale.

---

*"I don't build things that people don't need. And I don't build things people won't buy. Because in order for this to be sustainable, people need to buy. There needs to be funding for it."*
